extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. Transition t5 (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=10: sent := true) directly contradicts this: it sets sent=true while opted_out is true. So the only question is whether a state with opted_out=true and ticks>=10 is reachable, and whether t5 can fire there.

Witness execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
1. optout -> t1: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0
2-11. ten tick events -> t4 each time increments ticks (0->1->...->10, capped at 10) and sets sent=false; opted_out remains true. After the 10th tick: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
12. send_attempt -> guard (opted_out and ticks>=10) holds, so t5 is enabled and sets sent=true.

Resulting reachable state: opted_out=true, sent=true. This makes (opted_out implies not sent) false, violating the global invariant.

Note on nondeterminism: when opted_out=true and ticks>=10, both t3 (sent:=false) and t5 (sent:=true) are enabled for send_attempt. The property must hold on every reachable execution, so the existence of an execution that selects t5 is sufficient to violate it. The vacuously-or-otherwise-safe transitions t1, t2 (only when not opted_out), t3, and t4 all keep sent=false under opted_out; t5 is the lone offender, and it is reachable.
